coredata.dc:title: Design of new boron-doped carbon dots for nano-level assay of nebivolol in human plasma and commercial products; Application to greenness assessments

coredata.dc:description: Recently, nanomaterials have attracted a lot of attention due to their potential as effective fluorescent nano-sensor probes. They were distinguishing substitutes for other luminescent techniques, such as fluorescent dyes and luminous derivatization, because of their affordability, environmental friendliness, and special photocatalytic properties. In the suggested work, a straightforward method was used to create boron and nitrogen carbon dots (B@CDs) with a good quantum yield value of 31.15 % utilizing boric acid and di-sodium EDTA. For the purpose of characterizing QDs, a variety of instruments were employed, such as transmission electron microscopy, fluorescence spectroscopy, X-ray FTIR, and UV–VIS spectroscopy. Nebivolol (NEB) is a cardiovascular medication used globally to treat congestive heart failure and hypertension, is in the meantime. For this reason, a brand-new, environmentally friendly analytical technique was created to determine the amount of human plasma, uniformity test, and commercial nebivolol (NEB) tablets. After gradually adding NEB, the response of B@CQDs was enhanced at 438 nm (excitation at 371 nm). The calibration graph ranged between 20 and 500 ng mL−1 with a quantification limit (LOQ) of 2.50 ng mL−1 and a detection limit (LOD) of 0.82 ng mL−1.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Nebivolol
Greenness assessment
Fluorimetry
B@CQDs

Comparison study of the quantum approach versus the reported method

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: a) Reaction spectra B@CQDs with different concentrations of NED (20 to 500 ng mL−1), and b) high resolution transmission electron microscope (TEM) image for particle size determination of B@CQDs.
A) Dynamic light scattering (DLS) for particle size determination of B@CQDs and b) X-ray diffraction (XRD) spectrum for the synthesized quantum dots.
The Energy dispersive X-ray spectrometer (EDX) spectrum for B@CQDs.
A) Excitation dependent emission (red shift) for B@CQDs, and b) Effect of pH on the reaction between B@CQDs and NEB (200 ng mL−1).
A) Effect of B@CQDs concentration on the reaction with NEB and b) Effect of reaction time on the reaction between B@CQDs and NEB (200 ng mL−1).

A) The fluorescence calibration data for reaction of B@CQDs with different concentrations of NEB, and b) The selectivity of the proposed method for estimation of NEB with various interferences (200 ng mL−1).
Analytical parameters for determination of NEB using B@CQDs.

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: LOD: lower limit of detection; LOQ: lower limit of quantitation

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Accuracy and precision for determination of NEB using the proposed B@CQDs nanoprobes.

ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: Average of three determinations; RSD: Relative standard deviation.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Matrix effect of the b@cqds method for analysis of neb.
Application of the B@CQDs method for analysis of NEB in plasma.
Applicability of B@CQDs for content uniformity of NEB.
Greenness study of the proposed method for determination of NEB under different applications.
Analytical Eco-Scale for greenness assessments of the proposed method.

head.ce:title: Design of new boron-doped carbon dots for nano-level assay of nebivolol in human plasma and commercial products; Application to greenness assessments

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, College of Science, Princess Nourah bint Abdulrahman University, P.O. Box 84428, Riyadh 11671, Saudi Arabia
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry, College of Science, Princess Nourah bint Abdulrahman University, P.O. Box 84428, Riyadh 11671, Saudi Arabia

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, College of Sciences & Arts, King Abdulaziz University, Rabigh, Saudi Arabia
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry, College of Sciences & Arts, King Abdulaziz University, Rabigh, Saudi Arabia

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Pharmaceutical Analytical Chemistry, Faculty of Pharmacy, Al-Azhar University, Assiut Branch, Assiut 71524, Egypt
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Pharmaceutical Analytical Chemistry, Faculty of Pharmacy, Al-Azhar University, Assiut Branch, Assiut 71524, Egypt

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Spectrofluorimetric determination of nebivolol by using boron doped carbon dots as fluorescence probe.

Recently, nanomaterials have attracted a lot of attention due to their potential as effective fluorescent nano-sensor probes. They were distinguishing substitutes for other luminescent techniques, such as fluorescent dyes and luminous derivatization, because of their affordability, environmental friendliness, and special photocatalytic properties. In the suggested work, a straightforward method was used to create boron and nitrogen carbon dots (B@CDs) with a good quantum yield value of 31.15 % utilizing boric acid and di-sodium EDTA. For the purpose of characterizing QDs, a variety of instruments were employed, such as transmission electron microscopy, fluorescence spectroscopy, X-ray FTIR, and UV–VIS spectroscopy. Nebivolol (NEB) is a cardiovascular medication used globally to treat congestive heart failure and hypertension, is in the meantime. For this reason, a brand-new, environmentally friendly analytical technique was created to determine the amount of human plasma, uniformity test, and commercial nebivolol (NEB) tablets. After gradually adding NEB, the response of B@CQDs was enhanced at 438 nm (excitation at 371 nm). The calibration graph ranged between 20 and 500 ng mL−1 with a quantification limit (LOQ) of 2.50 ng mL−1 and a detection limit (LOD) of 0.82 ng mL−1.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Nebivolol ( Figure S1 , NEB) is a worldwide cardiovascular drug used to treat high blood pressure. It is a mildly vasodilating beta-blocker that is both competitive and cardio-selective [1]. NEB chemicaly is 1-(6-fluoro-3,4-dihydro-2H-chromen-2-yl)-2-[[2-(6-fluoro-3,4-dihydro-2H-chromen-2-yl)-2-hydroxyethyl]amino]-ethanol. Different reported methods were found for determining NEB as spectrophotometric [2–5], spectrofluorimetric [6], chromatographic [1,7–10] and electrochemical [11,12]. However, it was noted that the methodologies employed in earlier reports had certain drawbacks. For example, chromatographic procedures are widely used and have evolved into a standard piece of laboratory equipment, yet there are some noticeable financial disadvantages. First of all, the chromatographic system is more expensive than fluorimetric equipment to purchase and maintain [1,7–10]. The price of columns, solvents, mobile phases, and other consumables can also add up, especially for high sample throughput analysis. Moreover, the utilization of large amounts of organic solvents is less eco-friendly when compared to the proposed method. In addition, the spectrophotometric techniques [1,7–10]. Besides, various limitations were observed in the spectrophotometric and the fluorimetric reported methods [2–6] such as lack of sensitivity and using organic solvents increases the environmental pollution compared to the proposed method.

Small carbon nanoparticles, known as carbon quantum dots (CQDs), usually have a size between one and ten nanometers. Because of their small size and distinct quantum confinement effect, which results in discrete energy levels, they are regarded as zero-dimensional materials [13–18]. Additionally, carbon quantum dots have unique optical, electronic, and chemical properties [19–21]. They have a wide range of applications in optoelectronics, biomedicine, energy storage, and catalysis, and ongoing research continues to explore their potential in various fields [16,22–26]. In addition, boron introduces additional active site to improve the catalytic activity, sensitivity, and biocompatibility of the quantum dots [15].

Green chemistry could be a forward-thinking method of developing and engineering chemicals to mitigate their adverse effects on the environment. Utilizing green chemistry reduces the number of organic solvents, reagents, and energy utilized in analysis [27–29]. Besides, Green chemistry aims to replace hazardous chemicals with safer alternatives. By designing chemicals that are less toxic and pose fewer risks to human health, both workers and consumers can be better protected. This not only benefits individuals but also decreases healthcare costs related to chemical-related illnesses.

The aims of the new approach are based on applying the green chemistry method for the nano-level of NEB using a simple and selective method. So, the synthesis and application of environmentally friendly quantum dots offer promising avenues for improving the fluorescence detection of NEB in various applications. In addition, the proposed approach provides cost-effective and selective nanoprobe B@CQDs with particle size diameter of about 6.73 nm and a high quantum yield value of 31.15 %.

The authentic NEB powder and nevilob® 5 mg (Batch no. 2333170) were obtained from Marcyrl Pharmaceuticals, Egypt. Boric acid, disodium EDTA, methanol, ethanol, acetonitrile, sodium hydroxide, hydrochloric acid, phosphoric acid, and glacial acetic acid were obtained from El-Nasr Chem. Co, in Egypt. Stock solution from NEB was prepared by dissolving 10 mg authentic powder into 5 mL methanol with sonication [6]. Then the solution was further diluted and completed to the mark with distilled water.

For the catalysis of the green synthesis and solvent extraction for quantum dots, a microwave instrument (MFMI-100A, from Medfuture, Jinan, China) was used. Philips X-ray diffractometer (model PW3040/00) was utilized for scanning the powder X-ray from Philips Innovation lab (Eindhoven, Netherlands). Metrohm pH meter 780 from Metrohm (Herisau, Switzerland). A JEM-100CXII high-resolution transmission electron microscope (HR-TEM) was utilized for capturing TEM images manufactured by JEOL (Tokyo, Japan). ZEN 3600 Zetasizer equipment from Malvern Instruments was used for particle sizing (range = 0.3–10000 nm) using a dynamic light scattering technique (Malvern, UK). A fluorescence spectrometer, model FS2, with a 1.0 cm quartz sample cell and ozone-free xenon light source (150 W) was used during the experiment and was obtained from Sinco (Korea). Fourier-transform infrared (FTIR) model FT-08 was purchased from Lumex instruments (Wakendorf, Germany).

The QDs were prepared by microwave assistance method. Briefly, 500 mg of EDTA and 300 mg of boric acid were dissolved in ultrapure water (50 mL). The solution was added to the microwave (700 w) for 8 min (using a microwave conical flask) until brown color solution was formed. After filtration, the solution was sonicated for 20 min, centrifuged for 10 min (4000 rpm), and then filtered over a 0.45 µm cellulose membrane [17]. The produced solution was used for analysis and characterization.

Using 5-mL calibrated flasks, 1.0 of (0.14 mg mL−1) B@CQDs was included to 1.50 mL of BR pH 6.8, and then 1.0 mL NEB concentrations were mixed also to get final concentration (20 – 500 ng mL−1). The solution was completed to the 10 mL double distilled water, and after 7 min, the RFI was measured at λem 438 nm (Excitation: 371 nm).

Prior to examination, human plasma was kept at −20 degrees and was procured from the Egyptian blood bank. A mixture of 0.5 mL of drug-free plasma samples and 1.0 mL of methanol was added as a protein precipitating agent. The mixture was then centrifuged at 5000 rpm for 20 min. The samples were spiked with different concentrations of NEB in centrifugation tubes. The process was then carried out with the supernatant after 10 mL of ultra-pure water.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Application of B@CQDs to commercial tablets
ce:section.ce:para: Ten pharmaceutical tablets (nevilob 5 mg®) were weighed and coarsely ground in order to make them. A powdered tablet equivalent to 10 mg of NEB was added to a volumetric flask (100 mL) to reach a final concentration of 100 µg mL−1. The mixture was then sonicated for 5 min after 5 mL of methanol was added. After that, double-distilled water dilution and filtration were carried out. The uniformity testing was conducted using the previous procedure of commercial tables using USP [30,31].

The prepared B@CQDs exhibit a fluorescence emission spectrum with a maximum peak at 438 nm (excitation at 371 nm). It was found that the reaction of B@CQDs with NEB led to an enhancement in the fluorescence intensity of the prepared carbon dots with gradually increasing NEB concentrations at 438 nm, Fig. 1
a. Fluorescence enhancement mechanism can be explained by the presence of an electron donor–acceptor complex and hydrogen bonding between B@CQD and NEB, which results in an improved mechanism. The hydroxyl carboxyl of B@CQD and the fluorine atoms of NEB have two prominently active and strong adjacent hydrogen bonds [15,32,33]. The presence of –COO−, –OH, and trivalent −B groups led to viability to conjugate with the NEB [15,32,33]. The type of interactions OH-F and or OH-N and molecular symmetry are, in all cases, related to the strength of the hydrogen bond and the degree of resonance within the hydrogen bond system. In addition, the electron-accepting representative of trivalent charges is stabilized by the carbon skeleton of B CQDs and the electronic character of NEB, which can contribute to the conjugation of C = C bonds, as well as the effect of the synthesis of hydrogen bonds and electrons [34]. The effect of the donor–acceptor complex leads to an increase in the initiation of chromophores and become huge fluorophores [32,34].

The transmission microscope was utilized to examine the size diameter of B-CQDs. B@CQDs showed tiny particles with an average size of 6.73 nm as seen in Fig. 1 b. Another method, dynamic light scattering (DLS), was applied to confirm this average size using Zetasizer equipment (Fig. 2
a). DLS technique showed a mean particle size of 6.8 ± 0.21 nm, which agreed with TEM imaging. Fig. 2 a describes an average size of 6.8 ± 0.21 nm. In addition, Fig. 2 b describes a typical powder X-ray diffraction pattern (PXRD) of the resulting B@CQDs. Due to the small size of green synthesized B@CQDs, a diffraction peak centered at 25.88° and 51.60° was developed as previously reported one [15,32]. The functional groups for B@CQDs nanoparticles were studied using their Fourier-transform infrared (FTIR) spectrum ( Figure S2 ), as reported in the previous study [15]. Seven distinctive peaks can be observed at 3390, 2950, 1642, 1370, and 932 and 622 cm−1 that correspond to –OH, B-OH, C = O, B-C, B-O and B-O-B respectively ( Figure S2 ).

Energy dispersive X-ray (EDX) spectrum for B@CQDs was used to interpret B doping with carbon quantum dots (Fig. 3 ) as reported in a previously published method [15]. On closer inspection, it was discovered that distinct peaks for C, O, B could be seen (Fig. 3). The presence of sodium peak is due to using disodium EDTA for synthesis B@CQDs. The percentage of elements was found to be 22.34, 27.82, 44.26, 5.58 % for B, C, O and Na respectively.

The fluorescence Quantum yield (Q-Y) for the prepared B@CQDs was calculated utilzing Quinine sulphat (Q-Y 54 %) as [35,36]:
Q
BCQDs
=
Q
Quinin
×
F
NCQDs
F
Quinin
×
A
st
A
NCQDs
×
η
2
( N C Q D s )
η
2
( Q u i n i n )

Where, QBCQDs and Qquinin are fluorescence quantum yield for Boron doped carbon quantum dots and Q-Y for the standard solution of quinin sulphate as a reference respectively. (F) is the fluorescence intensity for quantum dots and quinin sulphate, A is the absorption and
η
is the refractive index. The quantum yields equal to 31.15 %.

The optical characteristics of the prepared boron carbon quantum dots were examined using UV/VIS measurements. As seen in Figure S3
, the UV spectrum of B@CQDs, which has two absorption peaks at wavelengths of 225 and 320 nm. Peaks are caused by function groups and the carbon skeleton of quantum dots, as reported in the previous study [15].

B@CQD excitation-dependent emission was investigated at various excitation wavelengths between 290 and 410 nm. Excitation-emission dependence of the carbon was confirmed by the observation that increasing excitation caused a red shift in the emission curves with quenching in RFI. Fig. 4
a.

The impact of the concentration of B@CQDs on the fluorescence intensity was examined using different concentrations. According to the results (Fig. 5
a) 0.14 mg mL−1 from B@CQDs produces maximum fluorescence intensity. The effect of interaction time effect was investigated up to 30 min (Fig. 5 b). The highest fluorescence was obtained from 6 min, and 7 min was chosen as the ideal time for reaction.

The suggested B@CQDs-method was validated for the dertermination of NEB according to ICH rules and US-FDA recommendations [37,38]. As shown in Table 1 , the calibration range for NEB determination using B@CQDs was from 20.0 to 500.0 ng mL−1. Fig. 6
a. The regression formula was observed: Y = 6.94 x + 923.55 with r2 0.9994. The limits of detection (LOD) and quantification (LOQ) values were determined as LOQ = 10 ϭ/ slope and LOD = 3.3 ϭ/ slope [39], where ϭ represents to standard deviation of the curve. The LOD and LOQ were observed to be 0.82 and 2.50 ng mL−1, respectively. The analytical parameters of the method and its related to the sensitivity are shown in Table 1.

The accuracy of the suggested technique was performed using 5 concentrations (20.0, 50.0, 100.0, 300.0, or 500.0 ng mL−1). The recovery results ranged from 98.33 % to 100.35 %, with relative SD values from 0.50 to 1.15, referring to the high accuracy method. Table 2 .

More investigation of intraday precision was performed using three concentrations (50.0, 100.0, and 200.0 ng mL−1), repeated three times. Additionally, 3 concentrations that were examined three times over three days were used to investigate the inter-day study with recovery ± RSD ranged from 98.00 ± 0.69 to 101. 03 ± 0.80 %. Table 2.

The selectivity of the creative technique B@CQDs was carried out to evaluate the interference from different sources. Various interferences such as sodium, potassium, cobalt, nickel, zinc, calcium, ferric, talc, starch and barium (200 ng mL−1) were utilized to estimate the selectivity of the quantum dots approach in different matrices, as shown in Fig. 6 b. The matrix effect was also studied on NEB determination, and the results indicated the absence of any effect of the matrix within the studied range with high precision. Table 3 shows a high % recovery, ranging from 96.00 % to 98.83 %.

In order to boost the applicability of the proposed nanoprobes, different matrices were examined for the quantification of NEB. Nebivolol was spiked at different concentrations in drug-free human plasma samples. Then, B@CQDs were used under the proposed analytical conditions for NEB quantification. The results (Table 4 ) show high recovery percentages (97.75 %) of studied drug using the proposed method from human plasma.

The proposed approach was used for the quantification of NEB in its commercial tablet and vial dosage forms (Nevilob® 5.0 mg/ tablet). The obtained percentage of recoveries ± RSD were discovered to be 101.77 ± 0.95.

A number of instruments for evaluating the environmental effects of analytical techniques have been made public. Such assessment helps in attaining sustainability goals through minimizing the environmental harm of the routine analytical chemistry activities. For instance, a standard HPLC system is able to generate about 0.5 L of organic solvents as waste on average per day [41].

The Greenness of the Analytical Procedure was evaluated using three analytical assessments: GAPI, AGREE and analytical auto-scale methods [28,42–44]. Table 6 describes three red zones in the GAPI pictogram; two of these red zones are within the sample pentagram and correspond to offline sampling using methanol as the organic solvent, which obviates the need for sample transportation and sample preparation is represented by the third red zone.

AGREE is another greenness evaluation tool that was applied for the suggested procedure [45]. Table 6 describes the AGREE pictograms of the proposed method and demonstrates the lowest environmental impact represented by numerical scores for all applications. The range of proposed methods is mostly green, except for the third, his GAC principle, which is related to unavoidable offline sampling, as highlighted in the description of the GAPI pictogram. The use of a low-power spectrofluorometer, its high throughput, and a simple sample preparation procedure that does not require derivatizing agents explain the better green behavior of the proposed methodology.

One of the initial metrics that was introduced in 2012 is the analytical eco-scale. This metric evaluates the analytical approaches by deducting penalty points from each step that does not adhere to the GAC's principles. As seen in Table 7 , high greenness effect of the suggested method.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Comparison study of the quantum approach versus the reported method
ce:section.ce:para:
The performance criteria of the fluorimetric sensor were compared with other methods, and the results are summarized in Table S2
. This table confirms that the sensor exhibits higher sensitivity compared to alternative approaches. Additionally, the newly developed sensor (B@CQDs) offers several advantages over previously documented techniques. These include the use of environmentally friendly solvents and the absence of extraction phases, which are present in other reported methods Table S2 .

A novel, simple, and cost-efficient method using microwave assistance was established and confirmed for producing boron and nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (B@CQDs). The entire synthesis process took an average of 35 min for all stages, including microwave thermolysis. This developed technique led to the creation of small-sized 6.73 nm and highly fluorescent green B@CQDs with a substantial quantum yield (31.15 %). These attractive characteristics and benefits position the resulting metal–carbon dots (M-CDs) as exceptional platforms for fluorescent probes in the quantification of NEB in human plasma with high percent of recovery equal to 97.75 % and 101.77 ± 0.95 for pharmaceutical dosage form. In addition, the eco-friendliness of this study was observed to align with green analytical chemistry.

Huda Salem AlSalem: Data curation, Funding acquisition, Methodology, Project administration, Writing – review & editing. Sara Naif Alharbi: Formal analysis, Resources, Writing – review & editing. Soha Talal Al-Goul: Writing – review & editing, Investigation, Software. Baher I. Salman: Software, Methodology, Investigation, Conceptualization, Validation, Writing – original draft, Visualization. Yasser F. Hassan: Validation, Methodology, Investigation, Conceptualization, Software, Visualization. Mohamed A. Abdel-Lateef: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Supervision, Project administration.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: Princess Nourah bint Abdulrahman University Researchers Supporting Project number (PNURSP2024R185), Princess Nourah bint Abdulrahman University, Riyadh, Saudi Arabia.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.saa.2024.124470.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary Data 1

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Quantitative determination of nebivolol from human plasma using liquid chromatography–tandem mass spectrometry
J. Chromatogr. B

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: J. Nandania, S. Rajput, P. Contractor, P. Vasava, B. Solanki, M. Vohra, Quantitative determination of nebivolol from human plasma using liquid chromatography–tandem mass spectrometry, Journal of Chromatography B, 923 (2013) 110-119.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Validated spectrophotometric methods for simultaneous determination of nebivolol hydrochloride and valsartan in their tablet
Microchem. J.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: A.H. Kamal, A.A. Marie, S.F. Hammad, Validated spectrophotometric methods for simultaneous determination of nebivolol hydrochloride and valsartan in their tablet, Microchemical Journal, 155 (2020) 104741.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Spectrophotometric method for the determination of nebivolol hydrochloride in bulk and pharmaceutical formulations
J. Chem.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: A.L. Rao, K. Rajeswari, G. Sankar, Spectrophotometric method for the determination of nebivolol hydrochloride in bulk and pharmaceutical formulations, Journal of Chemistry, 7 (2010) 445-448.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Simultaneous determination of Nebivolol hydrochloride and Valsartan in their binary mixture using different validated spectrophotometric methods
Spectrochim. Acta A Mol. Biomol. Spectrosc.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: E.M. Meselhy, A.A.A. Kheir, M.M. El Henawee, M.S. Elmasry, Simultaneous determination of Nebivolol hydrochloride and Valsartan in their binary mixture using different validated spectrophotometric methods, Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy, 230 (2020) 118083.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Determination of nebivolol hydrochloride and hydrochlorothiazide in tablets by first-order derivative spectrophotometry and liquid chromatography
J. AOAC Int.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: D.A. Shah, K.K. Bhatt, R.S. Mehta, S.L. Baldania, Determination of nebivolol hydrochloride and hydrochlorothiazide in tablets by first-order derivative spectrophotometry and liquid chromatography, Journal of AOAC International, 91 (2008) 1075-1082.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Second-derivative synchronous spectrofluorimetric determination of nebivolol hydrochloride and amlodipine besylate in their combined dosage form
Luminescence

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: F. Ibrahim, N. El‐Enany, S. Shalan, R. Abo Shabana, Second‐derivative synchronous spectrofluorimetric determination of nebivolol hydrochloride and amlodipine besylate in their combined dosage form, Luminescence, 30 (2015) 1011-1019.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Determination of nebivolol and valsartan in a fixed-dose combination by liquid chromatography
J. AOAC Int.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: A.S. Doshi, S.S. Bhagwan, T.N. Mehta, V.K. Gupta, G. Subaaiah, Determination of nebivolol and valsartan in a fixed-dose combination by liquid chromatography, Journal of AOAC International, 91 (2008) 292-298.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Development and validation of a stability-indicating column high-performance liquid chromatographic assay method for determination of nebivolol in tablet formulation
J. AOAC Int.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: P.K. Kachhadia, A.S. Doshi, H.S. Joshi, Development and validation of a stability-indicating column high-performance liquid chromatographic assay method for determination of nebivolol in tablet formulation, Journal of AOAC International, 91 (2008) 557-561.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Validation of a high-performance thin-layer chromatographic method, with densitometric detection, for quantitative analysis of Nebivolol hydrochloride in tablet formulations
JPC-J. Planar Chromatogr.-Modern TLC

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: T. Reddy, P. Devi, Validation of a high-performance thin-layer chromatographic method, with densitometric detection, for quantitative analysis of Nebivolol hydrochloride in tablet formulations, JPC-Journal of Planar Chromatography-Modern TLC, 20 (2007) 149-152.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Stability indicating RP-HPLC estimation of nebivolol hydrochloride in pharmaceutical formulations
Indian J. Pharm. Sci.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: D. Shah, K. Bhatt, R. Mehta, S. Baldania, T. Gandhi, Stability indicating RP-HPLC estimation of nebivolol hydrochloride in pharmaceutical formulations, Indian journal of pharmaceutical sciences, 70 (2008) 591.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Highly sensitive and selective electrochemical sensor based on high-quality graphene/nafion nanocomposite for voltammetric determination of nebivolol
Sens. Actuators B

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: E. Er, H. Çelikkan, N. Erk, Highly sensitive and selective electrochemical sensor based on high-quality graphene/nafion nanocomposite for voltammetric determination of nebivolol, Sensors and Actuators B: Chemical, 224 (2016) 170-177.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Development of electrochemical platform based on carbon nanotubes decorated with zirconium oxide nanoparticles for determination of nebivolol
Int. J. Electrochem. Sci.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: M. Sadiković, B. Nigović, Development of electrochemical platform based on carbon nanotubes decorated with zirconium oxide nanoparticles for determination of nebivolol, International journal of electrochemical science, 12 (2017) 9675-9688.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Quantification of tyramine in different types of food using novel green synthesis of ficus carica quantum dots as fluorescent probe
Luminescence
